items.question: Heap vs sorted list — when do you reach for each?
items.answer: **Heap:** O(1) peek, O(log n) push/pop, but no fast random access or arbitrary search. **Sorted list:** O(n) insert/delete in the middle (without sortedcontainers) but supports binary search and full ordered iteration. Use a heap when you only need the next-best; use sorted structures when ordering across the whole collection matters.

items.question: Heap vs BST?
items.answer: Both are binary trees, but a **heap** only orders parent-vs-children (root is min/max) and stores compactly in an array. A **BST** orders left-vs-right and supports search and range queries. Pick a heap for priority queues and top-k; pick a BST (or SortedDict) for ordered keyed access.

items.question: Why is heappush O(log n)?
items.answer: **Cause:** push appends to the end of the array and then sifts the new item up by swapping with its parent until the heap invariant holds. **Effect:** at most log n swaps (the tree's height), so each push is O(log n).

items.question: Why is bottom-up heapify O(n), not O(n log n)?
items.answer: **Cause:** half the nodes are leaves (no work), a quarter sit one level above (constant work each), an eighth sit two levels above (≤2 work each), and so on. **Effect:** summing the geometric series gives ∑ (n / 2^(h+1)) * h ≈ O(n) — much better than O(n log n).

items.question: What breaks if you push `(priority, message_object)` where message_object is unorderable?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** two pushes share the same priority, so Python compares the next tuple element — your custom object — and there's no `<` defined. **Consequence:** `heappush` raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances`. Insert a unique tie-breaker between priority and object: `(priority, next(counter), message)`.

items.question: What breaks if you use `heapq` for max-heap semantics without negating?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** push `(score, item)` expecting the largest score back from `heappop`. **Consequence:** `heappop` returns the *smallest* score — silently wrong rankings, wrong top-k, miscalibrated beam search. Either push `(-score, item)` and negate on pop, or use `heapq.nlargest`.
